label: Babylonian cosmology (Enūma Eliš)

scope.period: 2nd millennium BCE composition (dating debated); surviving tablets from c. 1200 BCE
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Scoped to the Enūma Eliš itself; wider Mesopotamian traditions (Sumerian cosmogonies, Atrahasis) tell different stories.

Single-source draft: sacred-texts.com (L.W. King translation) blocked fetching and Project Gutenberg holds no edition, so claims rest on one World History Encyclopedia article that embeds translation excerpts; a primary-text translation must be added at review.

No claims are recorded for ultimate_reality, constituents, self_and_consciousness, fundamental_problem, transformation, or epistemic_access: the epic narrates origins and order, not doctrine on these heads. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: Before heaven and earth there were only mingled primordial waters: 'in the beginning, there was only undifferentiated water swirling in chaos', which 'divided into sweet, fresh water, known as the god Apsu, and salty bitter water, the goddess Tiamat' — the gods themselves are generated, not eternal.

The ordered world is built from a slain god's body: 'Marduk defeats Quingu and kills Tiamat by shooting her with an arrow which splits her in two'; 'out of Tiamat's corpse, Marduk creates the heavens and the earth', and 'from her eyes flow the waters of the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers'.

Humanity is created to relieve the gods of labor: 'from his blood, Ea creates Lullu, the first man, to be a helper to the gods in their eternal task', as the epic states — 'Ea created mankind / On whom he imposed the service of the gods, and set the gods free'.
Calendar and celestial order are royal decrees of Marduk: 'he appointed the year, marked off divisions, And set up three stars each for the twelve months'.

Cosmic order is monarchic — established and maintained through Marduk's kingship over the gods, a theology bound to politics: 'the rise of the cult of Marduk is closely connected with the political rise of Babylon from city-state to the capital of an empire'.
claims.notes: The epic's date and setting are debated: the article both places composition 'long before the reign of Hammurabi of Babylon (1792–1750 BCE)' and notes the surviving tablets date to c. 1200 BCE; scholarly datings of the composition and of Marduk's elevation vary accordingly.

distinguishing_claims: The cosmos begins as undifferentiated waters; even the gods are generated
Heaven and earth are made from the divided corpse of a defeated deity
Humans exist to carry the gods' workload
Cosmic order mirrors and legitimates monarchic political order
common_confusions: Creation here is combat and craftsmanship upon pre-existing divine matter — not creation from nothing.
The Enūma Eliš is a Babylonian composition exalting Marduk; it does not speak for all Mesopotamian cosmogony.
Human purpose (serving the gods) inverts traditions where the cosmos exists for humanity's sake.

sources.title: Enuma Elish - The Babylonian Epic of Creation (World History Encyclopedia)
sources.author: Joshua J. Mark
sources.passage: In the beginning, there was only undifferentiated water swirling in chaos. [...] Out of this swirl, the waters divided into sweet, fresh water, known as the god Apsu, and salty bitter water, the goddess Tiamat. [...] Marduk defeats Quingu and kills Tiamat by shooting her with an arrow which splits her in two. [...] Out of Tiamat's corpse, Marduk creates the heavens and the earth. [...] From his blood, Ea creates Lullu, the first man, to be a helper to the gods in their eternal task. [...] Ea created mankind / On whom he imposed the service of the gods, and set the gods free. [...] He appointed the year, marked off divisions, And set up three stars each for the twelve months. [...] The rise of the cult of Marduk is closely connected with the political rise of Babylon from city-state to the capital of an empire. (Published 2018-05-04.)

review.notes: Drafted for D2-001 batch 4. Source URL fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Review needed: no human domain review yet; single tertiary source — add a primary-text translation (e.g. L.W. King or Lambert) at review; sacred-texts.com blocked fetching (403).
